stem: A group of [[n]] distinct people is to be divided into three labeled teams: Team A with [[a]] members, Team B with [[b]] members, and Team C with [[c]] members (with [[a]] + [[b]] + [[c]] = [[n]]). In how many ways can this assignment be made?
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[n]]! \); it forgets that members within a team are not ordered.
This divides by only two of the three team factorials.
This adds the factorials in the denominator instead of multiplying them.
This counts only the choice of Team A, ignoring how the rest split into B and C.
This is off from the correct count by the size of Team A.
This is off from the correct count by the size of Team C.

stem: In the expansion of \( (x+y+z)^{[[n]]} \), what is the coefficient of the term \( x^{[[p]]}y^{[[q]]}z^{[[r]]} \)?
solution: By the multinomial theorem, the coefficient of \( x^{[[p]]}y^{[[q]]}z^{[[r]]} \) in \( (x+y+z)^{[[n]]} \) is \( \dfrac{[[n]]!}{[[p]]!\,[[q]]!\,[[r]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( [[n]]! \), forgetting to divide out the repeated orderings within each variable's exponent.
This divides by only two of the three factorials.
This counts only the choice of positions for \( x \), ignoring how the rest split between \( y \) and \( z \).
This inverts the multinomial coefficient formula.
This is off from the correct value by one.
This is off from the correct value by one.

stem: In the expansion of \( ([[u]]x + [[v]]y + [[w]]z)^{[[n]]} \), what is the coefficient of the term \( x^{[[p]]}y^{[[q]]}z^{[[r]]} \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets the coefficient weights entirely and reports only the plain multinomial coefficient.
This forgets the multinomial coefficient factor entirely and reports only the product of the weight powers.
This forgets the weight power for \( z \).
This adds the weights raised to no power instead of multiplying by the correct powers of each.
This is off from the correct value by one.
This is off from the correct value by one.

stem: A group of [[n]] distinct people is split into three UNLABELED groups: two groups of size [[a]] each, and one group of size [[c]]. The two size-[[a]] groups are indistinguishable from each other (there is no 'first' or 'second' size-[[a]] group), while the size-[[c]] group is distinguishable by its different size. In how many ways can this partition be formed?
solution: Treating the two size-[[a]] groups as temporarily labeled gives \( \dfrac{[[n]]!}{[[a]]!\,[[a]]!\,[[c]]!} \) assignments, but swapping the two size-[[a]] groups gives the same partition, so divide by \( 2! \): \( \dfrac{[[n]]!}{[[a]]!\,[[a]]!\,[[c]]!\,2!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the two size-[[a]] groups as labeled (distinguishable), forgetting to divide by \( 2! \) for the indistinguishable pair.
This divides by only one factor of \( [[a]]! \) instead of \( ([[a]]!)^2 \).
This treats all [[n]] people and all groups as fully distinguishable, applying no divisions at all.
This divides by \( 3! \), as if all three groups (including the differently-sized one) were mutually interchangeable.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.
